topic1.title: Computer & System Fundamentals
topic1.concepts:
A CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the brain of a computer that executes instructions. In Azure integration contexts, the CPU matters because every compute resource — whether a Logic App Standard plan, Function App, or API Management gateway — runs on underlying virtual machines with allocated vCPUs. CPU-bound workloads are those spending most time on computation (e.g., parsing large XML payloads in BizTalk-style transformations), while I/O-bound workloads spend time waiting on network or disk (e.g., a Function App waiting for a Service Bus message or a database response). Understanding this distinction tells you when to scale horizontally versus vertically.

Memory (RAM) holds the working data a process is actively using. In integration architectures, memory becomes critical when processing large messages in-flight — a Logic App or Function App that streams through a 500MB blob will behave very differently from one deserializing it entirely into memory. Azure Function Apps running on a Consumption plan get 1.5GB RAM; Premium plans give more. Service Bus message size limits (256KB Standard, 100MB Premium) exist precisely because broker memory is shared across many clients. Throughput is the volume of work completed per unit time (messages/second, requests/second, MB/s), while latency is the time a single unit of work takes end-to-end. You can have high throughput with moderate latency; the two are distinct and often in tension.

Horizontal scaling (scaling out) means adding more instances of a component — more Function App instances, more Logic App workers, more Service Bus partitions. Vertical scaling (scaling up) means giving a single instance more resources — a larger VM SKU, more vCPUs, more RAM. Azure's serverless services like Function Apps (Consumption plan) and Logic Apps (Standard, multi-tenant) scale horizontally by default and automatically. This is why they're preferred in integration architectures: you get burst capacity without managing infrastructure. However, there are limits — Service Bus namespace throughput, APIM gateway capacity, and storage account IOPS are all ceilings you must design around.

Stateless vs stateful is a foundational design distinction. A stateless component holds no memory of prior interactions — each request carries all the context it needs. HTTP is inherently stateless. Azure Functions are designed to be stateless; if you need state across invocations, you externalize it (Cosmos DB, Redis, Service Bus session). Stateful components remember prior interactions — Durable Functions orchestrations maintain state checkpointed to Azure Storage. Logic Apps Standard workflows can be stateful (checkpointed to storage) or stateless (in-memory only, faster but no replay). In integration design, statelessness enables horizontal scaling; statefulness enables complex multi-step coordination.

The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of three properties simultaneously: Consistency (every read gets the most recent write), Availability (every request receives a response), and Partition tolerance (the system continues operating despite network splits). In practice, network partitions happen, so the real choice is between CP (consistent but may be unavailable during partition) and AP (available but may return stale data). Azure Service Bus is a CP system — it prioritizes consistency and durability over availability during failures. Azure Storage Queue is more AP-leaning. In integration architecture, CAP informs choices: if you need guaranteed ordering and exactly-once delivery, use Service Bus with sessions (CP). If you need maximum availability and can tolerate duplicates, Azure Storage Queues or Event Grid with at-least-once semantics may suffice.

terms.definition: The processor that executes instructions. Measured in cores/vCPUs; relevant when sizing compute for CPU-intensive workloads like transformation or encryption.
Fast working storage for active data. Affects how large a message payload can be processed in-memory before needing streaming or chunking.
Volume of work completed per unit time (requests/sec, messages/sec, MB/s). A Service Bus Standard namespace supports up to 1,000 brokered connections and ~256 KB messages.
Time elapsed for a single operation end-to-end. Affected by network distance, processing time, and queuing delays. P99 latency (99th percentile) is the senior metric to cite.
Adding more instances of a component. Azure Functions Consumption plan scales from 0 to 200 instances automatically. Stateless design is a prerequisite.
Giving a single instance more CPU/RAM. Done by changing SKU (e.g., B1 to P2V3 App Service plan). Has an upper ceiling; less elastic than horizontal.
No session memory between requests. Each call is self-contained. Enables horizontal scaling. Azure Functions are stateless by design.
Maintains context across interactions. Requires durable storage (Azure Storage for Durable Functions checkpoints, Logic Apps run history). Enables long-running workflows.
Consistency + Availability + Partition tolerance: a distributed system can guarantee at most two. Drives decisions on message broker selection.
Workload bottlenecked on network/disk rather than CPU. Most integration workloads are I/O bound (waiting on Service Bus, HTTP calls, database). Async programming patterns help.
Workload bottlenecked on computation. Examples: heavy JSON/XML transformation, cryptography, compression. Scaling compute (more vCPUs) is the remedy.
Mechanism where a downstream component signals it is overwhelmed, causing upstream to slow down. Service Bus auto-scaling and dead-letter queues implement a form of backpressure.

CAP POSITIONING:
  Service Bus   → CP  (consistent, durable, may queue during partition)
  Storage Queue → AP  (available, at-least-once, eventual consistency)
  Cosmos DB     → Configurable (5 consistency levels from Strong to Eventual)

interview_qa.q: When designing an Azure integration solution that processes 10,000 messages per hour, how do you decide between horizontal and vertical scaling?
interview_qa.a: I start by profiling the workload. If the Function App is CPU-bound — heavy XML-to-JSON transformation, crypto operations — I might first scale up the App Service Plan SKU to get more vCPUs per instance before scaling out. But for typical integration workloads that are I/O bound (waiting on Service Bus, downstream APIs, databases), horizontal scaling is almost always the right answer because you can add instances without changing code, and Azure Functions on Consumption or Premium plans do this automatically based on queue depth. For 10,000 messages/hour (~2.8/sec average), a single Premium EP1 Function App instance is usually sufficient, but I'd still design stateless — no in-memory state — so that if it needs to scale to 10 instances during peak, each instance is equivalent. I'd also check Service Bus namespace throughput limits and make sure I'm on the right tier.

interview_qa.q: Explain the difference between throughput and latency and give an example of a trade-off you've faced in an integration project.
interview_qa.a: Throughput is how much work you can process per unit time; latency is how long a single operation takes. They're related but not the same. In a BizTalk-to-Azure migration I worked on, we had a batch processing pattern where BizTalk received large EDI files, split them into individual transactions, and processed them sequentially. The throughput was low but the latency per file was actually acceptable. When we moved to Logic Apps with Service Bus, I initially made the mistake of increasing parallelism to maximize throughput — we went from processing 1 message at a time to 32 concurrent. Throughput went up 10x, but average latency per individual message went up because they were competing for the same database write endpoint. I had to back off to 8 concurrent workers, which hit our throughput SLA while keeping latency under 500ms P99. The lesson: these two metrics can conflict, and you have to know which one your SLA is actually measuring.

interview_qa.q: What does it mean for a Function App to be stateless, and what are the implications for Durable Functions?
interview_qa.a: A stateless Function App means each invocation is self-contained — the function reads its inputs, does its work, and returns output without relying on any in-memory data from a previous invocation. This is what makes them horizontally scalable: any instance can pick up any message. The implication is that if you need to coordinate a multi-step workflow — like an order processing flow that waits for payment confirmation, then triggers fulfillment, then sends notification — you can't store that intermediate state in a class-level variable. Durable Functions solve this by externalizing state to Azure Storage (queues and tables under the hood). The orchestrator function is essentially replayed from the event history each time it needs to resume, so it must be deterministic — no DateTime.Now, no random numbers, no direct HTTP calls inside the orchestrator. I've used Durable Functions for a loan approval workflow where each step had a different owner and SLA, and the state was checkpointed after every activity so we could resume from exactly the right point after a failure.

interview_qa.q: How does the CAP theorem influence your choice between Azure Service Bus and Azure Storage Queues?
interview_qa.a: CAP says a distributed system can guarantee consistency, availability, and partition tolerance — but not all three simultaneously. Service Bus prioritizes consistency and durability: messages are replicated across multiple broker nodes before acknowledgment, so you have strong delivery guarantees, FIFO within sessions, duplicate detection, and dead-lettering. This is a CP choice. Storage Queues are simpler and more available — they're backed by Azure Storage's geo-redundant infrastructure and designed for maximum throughput and availability, but with at-least-once delivery and no ordering guarantees. For integration scenarios where we're processing financial transactions or order state changes where duplicate processing would be harmful, I use Service Bus with duplicate detection enabled and sessions for ordering. For scenarios where volume is high, messages are idempotent by design (like telemetry ingestion), and availability matters more than ordering, Storage Queues or Event Grid work fine. The CAP trade-off is real and I've had to explain it to architects who defaulted to Service Bus everywhere.

interview_qa.q: How does memory management affect your design of a Logic App that processes large blob files?
interview_qa.a: Logic Apps Standard have a 104MB default payload limit and will attempt to load content into memory. If I'm processing a 500MB CSV or EDI file, loading it entirely into the workflow engine will cause out-of-memory failures or extremely slow processing. The right design is to use chunking — Logic Apps has built-in chunking support for certain connectors — or to process the file using a Function App with streaming APIs so the content never fully materializes in memory. In one project, we received large flat files via SFTP, uploaded them to Blob Storage, and triggered a Function App via Event Grid that streamed the blob through a parser line by line, pushing each record to Service Bus. The Logic App was only responsible for orchestrating the trigger and final notification, never touching the raw payload. This kept memory footprint low and let us process multi-GB files without infrastructure changes.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between a CPU-bound and an I/O-bound workload, and why does it matter for choosing an Azure compute tier?
interview_qa.a: CPU-bound workloads spend most of their time computing — things like XSL transformations, JSON schema validation, encryption/decryption, or machine learning inference. I/O-bound workloads spend most of their time waiting — for a Service Bus message to arrive, for an HTTP response from a downstream API, for a database query to return. Integration workloads are overwhelmingly I/O-bound. This matters for Azure compute tier selection because: for I/O-bound work, async programming and horizontal scaling with many smaller instances is optimal — Consumption plan Functions are cost-effective because they idle cheaply. For CPU-bound work, you want fewer but beefier instances — Premium plan with more vCPUs per instance. In a data enrichment pipeline I built, the Function was I/O-bound 95% of the time (waiting on a third-party API), but we had one step doing AES-256 encryption of PII fields that was CPU-bound. I split these into two separate Functions: the first on Consumption, the second on a dedicated Premium plan with CPU-optimized sizing.

interview_qa.q: Explain what P99 latency means and why it's more important than average latency for SLA design.
interview_qa.a: P99 latency is the 99th percentile: 99% of requests complete within this time. Average latency can be misleading because a small number of very slow requests can cause real user impact without moving the average much. If my integration service processes 10,000 requests and 100 of them take 30 seconds, but the average is 200ms, the average looks great but 1% of customers are experiencing terrible latency. Enterprise integration SLAs typically specify P95 or P99 because these capture the tail. In my Azure APIM work, I monitor P99 in Azure Monitor with custom alert rules. If P99 spikes above our SLA threshold, it usually indicates a downstream dependency is struggling — which I investigate via distributed traces in Application Insights. We fixed one incident where P99 was 15 seconds because a single downstream SAP endpoint was timing out for certain payload patterns — average was fine because 99% of calls bypassed that code path.

interview_qa.q: What happens in an Azure Function App when it scales out to multiple instances and one message is processed by two instances simultaneously?
interview_qa.a: This is the duplicate processing problem, and it's real with at-least-once delivery semantics. Azure Functions with a Service Bus trigger use message lock — the broker locks the message to the first instance that picks it up, and the lock is held until the function either completes (message deleted) or fails/times out (lock released, message redelivered). So normally, two instances won't process the same message simultaneously. But if processing takes longer than the lock duration (default 60 seconds, max 5 minutes in Premium tier) and the function doesn't renew the lock, the broker assumes the consumer is dead and redelivers the message — potentially to another instance. I've hit this with long-running enrichment steps. The fix: either design for idempotency — if a message is processed twice, the second time is a no-op — or use Durable Functions orchestration with proper checkpointing, or ensure the Service Bus lock renewal is implemented for long-running operations. In production, I always design for idempotency regardless, because network glitches and retries are a fact of life.

talking_points: In my BizTalk migration at EY, one of the first exercises was profiling whether each integration was CPU-bound or I/O-bound. BizTalk's execution model was single-threaded per orchestration, so we had hidden CPU bottlenecks in heavy map transforms. Moving to Function Apps with async/await patterns, we unlocked much higher I/O concurrency, and for the heavy transforms we parallelized across multiple function invocations.

I designed a scaling strategy for a high-volume EDI processing pipeline where we projected 50,000 messages per business day. I benchmarked throughput at P95 and P99 latency under load using Azure Load Testing, identified the Service Bus namespace as the throughput ceiling, and upgraded to Premium to get dedicated capacity. We avoided over-provisioning compute by letting auto-scale handle peaks.

I've explained CAP theorem to business stakeholders to justify why we couldn't have 'zero message loss AND always available AND real-time consistency' during a regional outage scenario. The decision was to accept eventual consistency with Service Bus geo-disaster recovery (secondary region replication) rather than try to build an impossible guarantee.

When migrating a stateful BizTalk orchestration to Azure, I evaluated Durable Functions versus Logic Apps stateful workflows. I chose Logic Apps Standard stateful for the business team's visibility — they could see run history in the portal — and reserved Durable Functions for developer-owned flows requiring more complex fan-out patterns.

I always instrument integration services with both throughput metrics (messages/sec via Azure Monitor) and latency metrics (P99 via Application Insights custom metrics). In one case this revealed that throughput was healthy but P99 had crept up — tracing showed a single downstream endpoint was degrading, not our service. That's the power of measuring both independently.

topic2.title: Networking & Web Protocols
topic2.concepts:
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of data communication on the web. It operates on a request-response model: a client sends a request with a method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), headers, and optionally a body; the server returns a response with a status code, headers, and optionally a body. HTTPS is HTTP over TLS (Transport Layer Security), which encrypts the channel. In Azure integration, virtually every service communicates over HTTPS — Logic Apps calling HTTP connectors, APIM receiving client requests, Function Apps exposing HTTP triggers. Understanding the protocol deeply matters because you'll be debugging 4xx/5xx errors, configuring timeouts, and designing retry policies.

REST (Representational State Transfer) and SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) represent two generations of web service design. SOAP is a protocol with strict XML envelopes, WSDL contracts, and defined fault structures. It was the standard in BizTalk's era and is still common in financial and enterprise systems. REST is an architectural style — not a protocol — using HTTP methods semantically (GET to retrieve, POST to create, PUT to replace, PATCH to partially update, DELETE to remove), with resources identified by URLs and typically JSON or XML bodies. REST is stateless by definition: each request contains all needed information. Azure's own APIs (ARM, Service Bus REST API, Blob Storage REST API) are all RESTful. APIM can expose SOAP backends as REST APIs through a transformation policy — this was a key pattern in our BizTalk migrations.

Webhooks are HTTP callbacks: instead of polling a server for events, you register an endpoint and the server pushes events to you when they occur. Azure Event Grid is webhook-based at its core — subscribers provide an HTTPS endpoint and events are pushed as JSON payloads. Logic Apps HTTP trigger is a webhook endpoint. The challenge with webhooks is reliability: if your endpoint is down when an event fires, you need the sender to retry. Event Grid provides retry policies with exponential backoff. In contrast, a polling pattern (your service calls the source repeatedly) is simpler but wastes resources and has higher latency. The choice between push (webhook/event-driven) and pull (polling) architectures is a recurring integration design decision.

The TLS handshake is the process establishing an encrypted connection before HTTP data flows. In a simplified sequence: (1) Client sends 'ClientHello' with supported cipher suites and TLS version. (2) Server responds with 'ServerHello', chosen cipher, and its certificate. (3) Client validates the certificate against trusted CAs. (4) Key exchange occurs (using asymmetric crypto like RSA or ECDH). (5) Both sides derive session keys (symmetric). (6) Data flows encrypted with the session key. In Azure integration: APIM terminates TLS from clients and can re-encrypt to backends (TLS offloading). Logic Apps and Function Apps always use HTTPS. Mutual TLS (mTLS) means both client and server present certificates — used for high-security B2B integrations. Azure APIM supports client certificate authentication policies to enforce mTLS.

DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable names (api.contoso.com) to IP addresses. In Azure integration, DNS is critical for: private endpoints (Azure Private DNS Zones map service names to private IPs within your VNet), custom domains on APIM and Function Apps (you add a CNAME or A record in your DNS zone), and service discovery. When a Logic App calls an API by hostname, Azure DNS resolves it. If you're using Private Endpoints, you must ensure the private DNS zone is linked to the VNet; otherwise name resolution falls back to public IPs and private endpoint connections fail. DNS TTL (Time To Live) affects how quickly changes propagate — relevant during blue/green deployments where you change DNS to switch traffic.

terms.definition: Semantic verb for an HTTP request: GET (retrieve), POST (create/submit), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove), HEAD (headers only), OPTIONS (capabilities).

2xx = success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content), 3xx = redirect (301, 302), 4xx = client error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests), 5xx = server error (500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable, 504 Gateway Timeout).
Representational State Transfer. An architectural style for distributed systems using HTTP semantics, stateless requests, resource-based URLs, and typically JSON/XML.
Simple Object Access Protocol. XML-based messaging protocol with strict envelope structure, WSDL contract, and WS-Security. Common in legacy enterprise and financial systems.
Web Services Description Language. XML document describing a SOAP service's operations, input/output message structures, and endpoint URL. Analogous to an OpenAPI spec for SOAP.
Industry standard for describing REST APIs in YAML/JSON. Azure APIM imports OpenAPI specs to auto-generate API definitions and documentation.
HTTP callback. A server pushes event notifications to a pre-registered HTTPS endpoint. Used by Event Grid, GitHub, Stripe, and many SaaS platforms to deliver events.
Cryptographic protocol that encrypts HTTP traffic (HTTPS). TLS 1.2 is minimum acceptable; TLS 1.3 is preferred. Involves a handshake to establish session keys.
Both client and server authenticate with X.509 certificates. Used for high-trust B2B integrations. Azure APIM can enforce client certificate policies.
Distributed system translating hostnames to IP addresses. Critical in Azure for private endpoints (Private DNS Zones), custom domains, and service discovery.
Azure-managed DNS zone resolving private endpoint hostnames to private IPs within a VNet. Required for private connectivity to Azure PaaS services.

Browser security mechanism. A browser will block JavaScript calling an API on a different origin unless the API responds with correct Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Relevant for APIM and Function App HTTP triggers called from web front-ends.
HTTP mechanism where client specifies accepted response formats via Accept header (e.g., application/json, application/xml). Server responds accordingly. APIM policies can transform between formats.

A method where calling it multiple times produces the same result as calling it once. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST is not idempotent by default — retrying a POST may create duplicate resources.

END-TO-END API COMMUNICATION FLOW

  CLIENT (browser / mobile / service)
      │
      │  HTTPS Request (TLS 1.2/1.3)
      │  GET /orders/123
      │  Headers: Authorization: Bearer <jwt>
      │           Accept: application/json
      │
      ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │        DNS RESOLUTION                   │
  │  api.contoso.com → 20.x.x.x (APIM IP) │
  │  (or private IP via Private DNS Zone)   │
  └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                     │
                     ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    AZURE API MANAGEMENT (APIM)          │
  │  - TLS Termination                      │
  │  - JWT Validation Policy                │
  │  - Rate Limiting Policy (429)           │
  │  - Transform: SOAP→REST (if needed)     │
  │  - Route to backend                     │
  └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                     │  HTTPS (re-encrypted)
                     ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    BACKEND: Logic App / Function App    │
  │  - Process request                      │
  │  - Call downstream services             │
  │  - Return 200 OK + JSON body            │
  └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                     │
                     ▼ (back up the chain)
  CLIENT receives HTTP 200
  Body: { "orderId": 123, "status": "shipped" }

TLS HANDSHAKE (simplified):
  Client → Server: ClientHello (TLS version, cipher list)
  Server → Client: ServerHello + Certificate
  Client: Validates cert against trusted CA roots
  Client → Server: Key exchange (ECDH public key)
  Both:  Derive symmetric session key
  Both:  Handshake complete → encrypted data flow begins

REQUEST TRACING HEADERS:
  X-Request-ID / x-correlation-id  → trace across hops
  X-Forwarded-For                   → original client IP (behind APIM/gateway)
  traceparent (W3C Trace Context)   → distributed tracing standard

interview_qa.q: Explain the difference between REST and SOAP and how you've used APIM to bridge between them.
interview_qa.a: REST is an architectural style that uses HTTP natively — resources are identified by URLs, operations are expressed through HTTP methods, and the payload is typically JSON. It's stateless and lightweight. SOAP is a protocol with its own envelope structure, always uses XML, and comes with WSDL for contract definition and WS-Security for authentication. It's verbose but formally specified — which is why financial services and legacy enterprise systems still use it. In my BizTalk migration work, we had SAP and Oracle backends that only exposed SOAP services. We used Azure APIM's 'Import SOAP' feature to generate an API definition, then applied a SOAP-to-REST transformation policy. The APIM policy injects the SOAP envelope, maps JSON request fields to XML elements, calls the backend, strips the SOAP envelope from the response, and returns clean JSON to the caller. This was a huge win — we didn't touch the backend at all, and consumers got a modern REST interface.

interview_qa.q: What HTTP status codes should a well-designed API return, and how does this affect Logic App retry policies?
interview_qa.a: A well-designed API should return: 200 OK for successful GET/PUT, 201 Created for successful POST creating a resource with a Location header pointing to the new resource, 204 No Content for successful DELETE, 400 Bad Request for malformed input with a problem-details body (RFC 7807), 401 Unauthorized when the caller lacks credentials, 403 Forbidden when credentials are valid but permission is denied, 404 Not Found for missing resources, 409 Conflict for state conflicts, 422 Unprocessable Entity for semantic validation failures, 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header, and 503 Service Unavailable during outages. In Logic Apps, the retry policy distinguishes between retryable and non-retryable status codes. By default, 408, 429, and 5xx trigger retries. 4xx client errors (400, 401, 403, 404) are not retried because retrying a bad request won't fix it. I've customized retry policies in Logic Apps to honor the Retry-After header from rate-limited downstream APIs, which prevents hammering a throttling endpoint.

interview_qa.q: Walk me through a TLS handshake and explain where it can fail in an Azure integration scenario.
interview_qa.a: The TLS handshake goes: client sends ClientHello with TLS version and supported cipher suites; server responds with ServerHello, the chosen cipher suite, and its X.509 certificate; client validates the certificate — it must be signed by a trusted CA, not expired, and the hostname must match the Subject Alternative Name; then key exchange happens, typically using ECDHE where both sides exchange public keys and derive a shared session key without transmitting it over the wire; finally, both sides send a Finished message and data flows encrypted. Where it fails in Azure: first, certificate expiry — I've seen Logic App connector calls fail because a backend had an expired cert and the connector correctly rejected it. I set up Azure Monitor alerts on Key Vault certificate expiry for all certs we manage. Second, self-signed or private CA certificates — if a backend uses an internal CA, you need to upload that CA cert to APIM's trusted CA store. Third, TLS version mismatches — some legacy backends only support TLS 1.0/1.1, which Azure services reject by default as they enforce TLS 1.2 minimum. We had to work with the backend team to upgrade their TLS configuration.

interview_qa.q: How do webhooks work in an Azure integration architecture, and what are the reliability challenges?
interview_qa.a: A webhook is an HTTP push mechanism: instead of your service polling for events, you register an HTTPS endpoint with the event source, and they POST event payloads to you when things happen. In Azure, Event Grid is the primary webhook broker — publishers send events to an Event Grid topic, and subscribers register HTTPS endpoints that get POSTed each event. Logic Apps HTTP triggers are webhook endpoints; you give the URL to an external system and it calls your Logic App when something happens. The reliability challenges are: first, delivery failure — if your endpoint is down, the sender may give up. Event Grid mitigates this with exponential backoff retries over 24 hours and a dead-letter blob container. Second, out-of-order delivery — Event Grid delivers at-least-once, not in-order. You must design consumers to be idempotent and handle ordering at the application layer if needed. Third, security — anyone who knows your webhook URL can POST to it. Azure Event Grid subscription validation (sends a validation event you must ACK) and HMAC signature validation headers mitigate this. I always validate the signature on incoming webhook payloads before processing.

interview_qa.q: Explain DNS and Private DNS Zones in the context of Azure Private Endpoints for Service Bus.
interview_qa.a: When you create a Service Bus namespace with a private endpoint, Azure assigns it a private IP within your VNet. But the namespace's public hostname (mybus.servicebus.windows.net) still resolves to a public IP via normal DNS. To make it resolve to the private IP from within your VNet, you need an Azure Private DNS Zone — specifically privatelink.servicebus.windows.net — with an A record mapping the private endpoint's name to its private IP. You then link this Private DNS Zone to your VNet. After that, any resource within the VNet that resolves mybus.servicebus.windows.net gets the private IP, and traffic never leaves the Microsoft backbone. The failure mode I've debugged: you create the private endpoint and the DNS zone, but forget to link the DNS zone to the VNet where your Function App or Logic App lives. Name resolution falls back to public DNS, returns the public IP, and the connection is blocked because you've set the namespace to deny public access. The fix is always to check the Private DNS Zone > VNet links, and use 'nslookup' from within the VNet (via a test VM or Azure DNS troubleshooting) to verify the private IP is returned.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between authentication (401) and authorization (403), and how should your API design communicate these?
interview_qa.a: Authentication is proving who you are — 401 Unauthorized means the request has no valid credentials. Authorization is proving you're allowed to do something — 403 Forbidden means you're authenticated but not permitted to perform this action. The HTTP spec names 401 'Unauthorized' which is confusing, but the semantic is 'unauthenticated'. A key security consideration: you sometimes want to return 404 Not Found instead of 403, to avoid leaking information about the existence of a resource to unauthorized callers — this is called security through obscurity and is appropriate for high-sensitivity data. In APIM, the validate-jwt policy returns 401 when the JWT is missing or invalid, and a send-one-way-request or check-header policy can return 403 when the caller lacks a required claim. I always document both in OpenAPI spec with appropriate schema for the error response body using RFC 7807 Problem Details format.

interview_qa.q: How do you handle an API that returns 429 Too Many Requests in a Logic App workflow?
interview_qa.a: 429 indicates rate limiting. A well-behaved API includes a Retry-After header specifying how many seconds to wait. In Logic Apps, I configure the retry policy on the HTTP action: set type to 'Fixed Delay' or 'Exponential' and set the retryable status codes to include 429. I also set a maximumDelay that respects the API's documented rate limit reset window. But if the downstream API's rate limits are tight and my volume is high, I add an intermediary: put requests on a Service Bus queue and process them with a Function App that uses a token bucket or sliding window rate limiter before forwarding to the rate-limited API. This is called the throttling gateway pattern. I've implemented this for an integration with a third-party financial data API that had a 60 requests/minute limit but we needed 1000 requests/minute throughput — the queue absorbed bursts and the Function processed at exactly the allowed rate.

interview_qa.q: Explain correlation IDs and distributed tracing, and how you implement them across a Logic App → Service Bus → Function App chain.
interview_qa.a: A correlation ID is a unique identifier generated at the entry point of a request and propagated through every hop, so you can stitch together all the logs and traces from a single end-to-end transaction. Without it, debugging failures across services is nearly impossible. In Azure, the W3C Trace Context standard defines the traceparent header format. Application Insights supports this natively — if a Function App receives an HTTP request with a traceparent header and has App Insights instrumented, it automatically continues the distributed trace. For Service Bus, you set the Correlation ID property on the message when enqueuing from Logic Apps, and the consuming Function App reads it and sets it as the App Insights operation ID. In Logic Apps, the run ID serves as a correlation handle, but I also log a custom x-correlation-id from the trigger to Application Insights custom events at each step. I've built a Kusto query in Log Analytics that joins Logic App run logs with Function App traces via this correlation ID to give a single timeline view of an end-to-end transaction — invaluable for SLA reporting and incident investigation.

talking_points: In my BizTalk migration project, we had 15+ backend SOAP services. Rather than rewriting them, I used APIM's SOAP import and transformation policies to give all consumers a consistent REST/JSON interface. This decoupled consumer teams from the legacy backend contracts and let us migrate backends incrementally without breaking consumers.

I've debugged TLS failures in Logic App connections probably a dozen times — usually expired certificates or missing private CA in the trust store. Now I proactively set Azure Monitor alerts on Key Vault certificate expiry at 60 and 30 days, and I document every TLS dependency in our integration runbook.

I implemented a webhook security model for an Event Grid subscriber: HMAC-SHA256 signature validation on every incoming payload, with the shared secret stored in Key Vault and loaded via Managed Identity. If the signature doesn't match, we return 400 and log the event — never process unvalidated webhook payloads.

On a project where the client asked why their Function App couldn't reach a private Service Bus endpoint, the root cause was always DNS — the Private DNS Zone wasn't linked to their VNet. I've made a checklist: create private endpoint, create Private DNS Zone, link zone to VNet, verify with nslookup from within VNet. In that order, every time.

I standardized error responses across all our APIs using RFC 7807 Problem Details, implemented via an APIM error-handling policy. Every error — 400, 401, 403, 404, 500 — returns a JSON body with type, title, status, detail, and a traceId. This dramatically reduced the time developers spent debugging integration failures.

topic3.title: Identity & Security
topic3.concepts:
OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework — not an authentication protocol — that allows a client application to obtain limited access to an HTTP service on behalf of a resource owner (user) or on its own behalf. The key flows relevant to Azure integration are: Client Credentials Flow, used for machine-to-machine (M2M) communication where there's no user involved (e.g., a Function App calling an API); Authorization Code Flow with PKCE, used when a human user is delegating access (e.g., a user logs into a web app that then calls an API on their behalf); and On-Behalf-Of (OBO) Flow, where a middle-tier service calls a downstream service using the user's identity. For integration services, Client Credentials is the dominant pattern — your Logic App or Function App has its own application identity and requests tokens for specific scopes from Azure Entra ID.

JWT (JSON Web Token) is the token format used by Azure Entra ID for access tokens. A JWT has three Base64URL-encoded sections separated by dots: Header (algorithm and token type), Payload (claims — standard ones like iss/issuer, sub/subject, aud/audience, exp/expiration, and custom ones added by Entra ID like roles, scp/scopes, appid, tid/tenant ID), and Signature (cryptographic signature allowing the recipient to verify the token without calling the issuer). The signature uses the issuer's private key (RSA or EC); recipients validate it using the issuer's public key fetched from the JWKS URI (Entra ID publishes this at a well-known endpoint). Never trust a JWT without validating the signature, the issuer claim, the audience claim, and the expiration. In APIM, the validate-jwt policy automates this.

Azure Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is Microsoft's cloud identity provider. The core objects are: Tenant (a dedicated instance of Entra ID for your organization, identified by tenant ID GUID), App Registration (represents your application — defines its identity, permissions, and credentials; has an Application ID/Client ID), Service Principal (the per-tenant instance of an App Registration; when you register an app in your tenant, a Service Principal is created automatically in that tenant; it's the actual identity that receives role assignments and can log in), Enterprise Application (same as Service Principal but the portal view for configuring SSO and user consent), and Managed Identity (a special service principal whose credentials are managed by Azure — no secret rotation needed).

Managed Identity eliminates the credential management problem. When you enable System-Assigned Managed Identity on a resource (Logic App, Function App, APIM), Azure creates a Service Principal in Entra ID whose lifecycle is tied to that resource — when you delete the resource, the identity is deleted. You then grant that identity RBAC roles or access policies on other resources (e.g., grant the Function App's identity 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' on a storage account). The Function App obtains tokens at runtime from the Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint without any credentials in code or config. User-Assigned Managed Identity is a standalone identity resource you create separately and can assign to multiple resources — useful when you want multiple resources to share the same identity or when you need the identity to exist before the compute resource.

Azure Key Vault is the centralized secret store for Azure integrations. It stores three types of objects: Secrets (arbitrary strings — connection strings, API keys, passwords), Certificates (X.509 certificates with private keys, including auto-rotation through integrated CAs), and Keys (cryptographic keys for encryption/decryption and signing, HSM-backed for compliance). Access is controlled through Key Vault Access Policies (legacy) or Azure RBAC (preferred — 'Key Vault Secrets User' role). Integration services (Logic Apps, Function Apps, APIM) access Key Vault via Managed Identity, so no secrets live in application config. Logic Apps Standard uses Key Vault references in connection strings. Function Apps use Key Vault references in App Settings (syntax: @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=...)). APIM uses named values that reference Key Vault secrets.

terms.definition: Authorization framework for delegating access. Defines flows (grants) for different scenarios: Client Credentials (M2M), Authorization Code (user delegation), Implicit (legacy browser), Device Code (headless), On-Behalf-Of (token exchange).

OAuth flow where a client uses its own identity (client_id + client_secret or certificate) to obtain an access token. No user involved. The primary pattern for service-to-service integration in Azure.
Compact, self-contained token format: Header.Payload.Signature (Base64URL encoded). Contains claims about the subject. Signature allows verification without calling the issuer.

Key-value pairs in the JWT payload. Standard: iss (issuer), sub (subject), aud (audience), exp (expiration), iat (issued at). Entra ID adds: tid (tenant), appid (client app), scp (delegated scopes), roles (app roles).
JSON Web Key Set endpoint published by identity providers (Entra ID: https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/discovery/v2.0/keys). Recipients fetch public keys here to verify JWT signatures.
Microsoft's cloud identity and access management service. Provides authentication, authorization, and identity management for Azure resources and applications.
A dedicated, isolated instance of Entra ID. Identified by tenant ID (GUID) or domain (contoso.onmicrosoft.com). All identities, app registrations, and policies live within a tenant.

The global application object in Entra ID. Defines the application's identity (Application ID/Client ID), API permissions it requests, exposed scopes, redirect URIs, and credentials (secrets or certificates).

The per-tenant instance of an App Registration. This is the actual security identity that authenticates and receives role assignments. Created automatically when an app is registered or consented to in a tenant.

An automatically managed service principal. System-Assigned: lifecycle tied to the resource. User-Assigned: standalone resource shared across multiple compute resources. No credential management needed.

Azure's authorization model. Assigns roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader, and built-in data-plane roles) to security principals (users, groups, service principals, managed identities) at a scope (management group, subscription, resource group, resource).
Managed HSM-backed service for storing secrets, certificates, and cryptographic keys. Supports access via RBAC or access policies. Integrates with Managed Identity for secretless authentication.

A syntax in Azure App Service / Function App application settings that resolves a secret from Key Vault at runtime: @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://...). The compute resource must have Key Vault Secrets User RBAC role.

In OAuth 2.0, a string identifying a permission. For Azure resources: https://management.azure.com/.default, https://servicebus.azure.net/.default. Clients request scopes; the token's scp or roles claims confirm what was granted.

Entra ID policy engine that enforces additional requirements before granting tokens: MFA, compliant device, specific IP ranges, specific app. Can block or require step-up auth for sensitive integration scenarios.

  'Storage Blob Data Reader'──▶  Storage Account (Scope)

interview_qa.q: Explain OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow and when you use it in Azure integration vs Authorization Code flow.
interview_qa.a: Client Credentials is the machine-to-machine flow: the client (a Function App, Logic App, or background service) authenticates directly with Entra ID using its own client_id and either a client_secret or a certificate, specifies the target resource's scope, and receives an access token. There is no user in this flow. I use this when a service is acting on its own behalf — calling another API as the application identity. Authorization Code with PKCE is used when a human user is involved — they need to consent to the application accessing resources on their behalf. In integration architecture, the vast majority of service-to-service calls use Client Credentials. The exception is On-Behalf-Of flow, which I use in middle-tier services: if an API receives a token representing User A and needs to call a downstream service, it can exchange that token for a new token that still represents User A — preserving the user's identity and their specific permissions throughout the call chain. This matters when you have row-level security based on user identity in a downstream database.

interview_qa.q: What does it mean to validate a JWT, and what claims must you always check?
interview_qa.a: Validating a JWT means verifying both its authenticity and its validity. Authenticity: verify the cryptographic signature using the issuer's public key fetched from the JWKS endpoint. If the signature doesn't verify, the token has been tampered with and must be rejected. Validity — the claims you must check: (1) iss (issuer) — must match the expected issuer URL for your tenant, preventing tokens from a different Entra ID tenant from being accepted; (2) aud (audience) — must match your application's App ID URI or client ID, preventing tokens issued for a different API from being accepted (confused deputy attack); (3) exp (expiration) — must be in the future, with a small clock skew tolerance; (4) nbf (not before) — if present, token must not be used before this time. In APIM's validate-jwt policy, I specify the openid-config URL for automatic key discovery, the required audience, and optionally required claims. I've seen bugs where developers only checked the signature but not the audience — this allowed cross-service token reuse in a multi-API environment.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between an App Registration and a Service Principal in Azure Entra ID?
interview_qa.a: An App Registration is the global application definition — it lives in one tenant and is the 'blueprint' that defines the application's identity. It has the Application ID (Client ID), and you configure things like redirect URIs, API permissions the app requests, scopes it exposes, and credentials (secrets or certificates) on the App Registration. A Service Principal is the per-tenant instance of that application. When you register an app in Tenant A, a Service Principal is automatically created in Tenant A. If another organization in Tenant B installs or consents to the same multi-tenant app, a Service Principal is created in Tenant B as well. RBAC role assignments are made to Service Principals, not App Registrations. When I give a Logic App's identity access to a Service Bus namespace, I'm assigning the role to the Service Principal of that Logic App's App Registration. For Managed Identity, Azure creates both the App Registration and Service Principal automatically and manages their lifecycle — I never have to handle secrets.

interview_qa.q: Compare System-Assigned vs User-Assigned Managed Identity — when would you choose each?
interview_qa.a: System-Assigned Managed Identity is created automatically when you enable it on a resource and its lifecycle is tied to that resource — delete the resource, delete the identity. It's 1:1 with the resource. Use it for simple cases where one resource needs its own unique identity with no sharing. User-Assigned Managed Identity is a standalone Azure resource you create independently and can assign to multiple compute resources. Use it when: (1) you want multiple resources to share the same identity — for example, multiple Function App instances in different regions all using the same managed identity to access Key Vault, so you only need one RBAC assignment; (2) you need the identity to persist independently of the compute resource — useful in deployment scenarios where resources are recreated; (3) you need the identity configured before the compute resource exists (you can pre-assign RBAC to a user-assigned identity, then attach it to the Function App at deployment). In practice I often use User-Assigned for production workloads because infrastructure-as-code is cleaner — the identity is a separate resource with its own lifecycle, and I assign it consistently across environments.

interview_qa.q: How does Azure APIM validate JWTs, and what happens when validation fails?
interview_qa.a: APIM uses the validate-jwt policy in the inbound pipeline, before the request reaches the backend. The policy is configured with the openid-config URL (Entra ID's well-known endpoint, which includes the JWKS URI for public key discovery), the required audiences (your API's App ID URI), and optionally required claims or roles. When a request arrives: APIM extracts the JWT from the Authorization header (Bearer scheme), fetches the signing keys from the JWKS URI (cached and refreshed periodically), verifies the signature, checks issuer, audience, and expiry claims. If any check fails, APIM immediately returns 401 Unauthorized to the caller without forwarding the request to the backend — the backend never sees unauthenticated requests. I also add a check-header policy for required custom headers and rate-limit policies, all in the inbound pipeline. The backend can then trust the caller's identity from the claims in the token. For multi-tier authorization (checking roles within claims), I use a policy condition checking the @(context.Request.Headers['Authorization'])...

interview_qa.q: Walk me through how you would securely provide a connection string to a Function App without it ever appearing in application settings in plaintext.
interview_qa.a: The pattern is: Key Vault + Managed Identity + Key Vault Reference. First, enable System-Assigned Managed Identity on the Function App. Second, go to the Key Vault and assign the 'Key Vault Secrets User' RBAC role to the Function App's managed identity at the Key Vault scope (using Azure RBAC, not access policies — RBAC is the modern approach). Third, store the connection string as a secret in Key Vault. Fourth, in the Function App's Application Settings, add the setting with value @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://<vault>.vault.azure.net/secrets/<secret-name>/<optional-version>). Azure App Service resolves this reference at startup and injects the actual secret value into the environment. The connection string never appears in ARM templates, deployment pipelines, or portal settings. If the secret rotates in Key Vault, the Function App picks up the new version on next restart (or immediately if you omit the version and use the 'latest' reference). I've set this up in Bicep templates where Key Vault secret URIs are passed as secure parameters and the RBAC assignment is part of the same deployment.

interview_qa.q: What is the principle of least privilege and how do you implement it in Azure integration architecture?
interview_qa.a: Least privilege means granting identities only the minimum permissions required to perform their function — nothing more. In Azure integration, I implement this through granular RBAC role assignments rather than using Contributor or Owner. A Function App that reads from Service Bus gets 'Azure Service Bus Data Receiver' on the specific queue, not on the entire namespace. A Logic App that reads blobs gets 'Storage Blob Data Reader' on the specific container, not the storage account. A deployment pipeline gets 'Contributor' on the specific resource group, not the subscription. I assign roles at the narrowest scope possible. In Key Vault, I use 'Key Vault Secrets User' for services that only need to read secrets, not 'Key Vault Contributor' which allows managing the vault itself. I review role assignments quarterly and remove any that are no longer needed. I've introduced this as a security standard in my team by making Managed Identity with least-privilege RBAC the required pattern in our Bicep module library — defaults to the minimum role, and any broader access requires a documented justification.

interview_qa.q: Explain what a confused deputy problem is in the context of OAuth and Azure APIs.
interview_qa.a: The confused deputy problem occurs when a legitimate service is tricked into acting on behalf of an attacker because it has more privilege than the attacker. In OAuth terms: if API-A accepts any valid Entra ID token as proof of authorization, an attacker can obtain a token for API-A's resource URI (by logging into any other service that trusts the same Entra ID) and use it to call API-A with elevated privileges — the 'deputy' (API-A) is confused because it can't distinguish legitimate callers from attackers. The fix is audience validation: API-A must require the JWT's aud claim to exactly match its own App ID URI. A token issued for API-B's audience will fail validation at API-A even if it's signed by the same Entra ID tenant. I've found misconfigured APIM policies that skipped audience validation during testing and forgotten to re-enable it — in my security review checklist, audience validation is a mandatory check.

talking_points: On our BizTalk migration project, we replaced all hard-coded credentials in XML config files with Managed Identity authentication. Every service now authenticates to Service Bus, Key Vault, and Storage using its assigned managed identity — no secrets in code, config, or deployment pipelines. Our security audit score improved significantly and secret rotation became a non-event.

I implemented a token caching pattern in our Function Apps because they were calling Entra ID for a new token on every invocation, which added 200-400ms of latency per call. Using Azure.Identity's DefaultAzureCredential with the built-in token cache, we reduced that to near-zero for the cache-hit path. Token cache expiry is handled by the SDK automatically.

I configured APIM's validate-jwt policy for three different APIs in the same gateway, each with different audiences and required roles. I used APIM named values to parameterize the tenant ID and authority URL so the policy code is the same across environments — only the named values differ between dev/staging/prod.

When a team asked me why their Function App was getting 403 from Key Vault even with Managed Identity enabled, the answer was always the same: they enabled the identity but forgot to create the RBAC role assignment. Now I include the role assignment in our Bicep module as a required sibling resource to the compute resource — you can't deploy without it.

I've trained my team on JWT debugging using jwt.ms — paste the token, see all claims immediately. The most common bugs I find: aud claim doesn't match the API's registered App ID URI, exp claim is in the past (clock skew), or the roles claim is missing because the app role wasn't assigned to the service principal.

topic4.title: Azure Core Architecture
topic4.concepts:
Azure's management hierarchy has four levels. Management Groups are containers for subscriptions — you can apply governance policies and RBAC assignments at the management group level that cascade to all subscriptions within them. Large enterprises use management groups to separate environments (production vs non-production), regions, or business units. Subscriptions are billing and isolation boundaries — resources within a subscription share a credit limit, and RBAC assignments at the subscription level apply to all resource groups within it. Resource Groups are logical containers for resources that share a lifecycle — you typically group resources for an application or workload together, deploy them together, and delete them together. Resources are the actual Azure services: a Function App, Service Bus namespace, Key Vault, etc. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for designing governance, cost allocation, and access control.

Azure Regions are geographic locations where Azure data centers are located (East US, West Europe, Southeast Asia, etc.). Each region is generally an independent failure domain. Azure Availability Zones (AZs) are physically separate data centers within a region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking. When you deploy a resource as zone-redundant, it's replicated across AZs — a single data center failure doesn't affect the service. Not all Azure services support AZs (it depends on the region and service tier). Service Bus Premium supports zone redundancy. Azure Functions Premium and Dedicated plans can be zone-pinned. Logic Apps Standard (App Service plan) supports zone redundancy on Premium and Isolated SKUs. When designing for high availability within a region, use AZs. When designing for disaster recovery across regions, use geo-paired regions or active-active deployments.

High Availability (HA) and Disaster Recovery (DR) are distinct concepts. HA is about preventing downtime due to expected failures — hardware failures, rolling updates, single AZ outage. HA is achieved through redundancy within a deployment (multiple instances, zone redundancy, load balancing). Azure SLAs for most PaaS services assume HA at the service level (e.g., Service Bus Premium is 99.9% SLA with zone redundancy). DR is about recovering from catastrophic failures — an entire region going dark, a data corruption event, accidental deletion. DR involves replication to a secondary region, defined RTO (Recovery Time Objective — how long to recover) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective — how much data loss is acceptable). For integration services, Service Bus Geo-DR (metadata replication) and active-active deployments with Azure Traffic Manager or Front Door are common DR patterns.

Service Level Agreements (SLAs) in Azure are contractual commitments about uptime. They're expressed as percentages: 99.9% = up to ~8.7 hours/year downtime, 99.95% = ~4.4 hours/year, 99.99% = ~52 minutes/year. Composite SLA is the SLA of a multi-component system — it's the product of all component SLAs (0.999 × 0.999 × 0.999 = 0.997, so three 99.9% services give you a composite 99.7%). This is why integration architectures with many sequential dependencies need careful SLA analysis. Adding redundancy (active-active across regions) raises your composite SLA. Understanding SLAs helps you justify architecture choices to stakeholders.

Azure Resource Manager (ARM) is the management plane — the API layer through which all resource creation, modification, and deletion flows. When you deploy resources via the portal, CLI, PowerShell, Terraform, or Bicep, all requests ultimately go through ARM. ARM provides: consistent API authentication (all management calls use Entra ID tokens), RBAC enforcement (ARM checks your permissions before taking action), tagging (key-value metadata on any resource for cost allocation and governance), deployment templates (ARM JSON or Bicep DSL for infrastructure as code), resource locking (prevent accidental deletion of critical resources), and policy enforcement (Azure Policy evaluates resources against rules defined at management group or subscription scope).

terms.definition: Container for subscriptions. Enables governance at scale — Azure Policy and RBAC applied at management group cascade down. Root management group exists for every Entra ID tenant.
Billing boundary and trust boundary for Azure resources. Contains resource groups. Azure limits (quotas) apply per subscription. A single Entra ID tenant can have multiple subscriptions.
Logical container for resources that share a lifecycle. Region-associated (metadata stored in a region) but can contain resources from any region. Unit of deployment and deletion.
Geographic area containing one or more data centers. Examples: eastus, westeurope, southeastasia. Resources are deployed to specific regions for data residency and latency.
Physically separate data center within a region with independent power, cooling, networking. Zone-redundant resources are replicated across AZs, surviving single AZ failure.
Microsoft-defined pairs of regions that are geo-replicated and updated in sequence (e.g., East US ↔ West US, North Europe ↔ West Europe). Used for geo-redundant replication.
Design goal of preventing downtime from expected component failures. Achieved via redundancy (multiple instances), zone redundancy, load balancing. Measured by uptime SLA.
Capability to recover from catastrophic events (region failure, data loss). Defined by RTO and RPO. Implemented via geo-replication, backups, and failover procedures.
Maximum acceptable time to restore service after an outage. Drives decisions on hot/warm/cold standby deployment patterns.
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time. An RPO of 0 means no data loss (synchronous replication required). An RPO of 1 hour means up to 1 hour of data loss is acceptable.
Microsoft's contractual uptime commitment for a service at a given tier. Breaches result in service credits. Composite SLA = product of all component SLAs.
Management plane API for all Azure resources. Authentication via Entra ID. All portals, CLIs, SDKs, and IaC tools call ARM. Enforces RBAC, policies, and resource locks.
Governance service that evaluates resources against rules. Can audit, deny, or automatically remediate non-compliant resources. Applied at management group, subscription, or resource group scope.
ARM feature preventing accidental modification (CanNotDelete) or any changes (ReadOnly) to critical resources. Applied by authorized users; must be removed before modifying/deleting the resource.
Infrastructure-as-code languages for declaring Azure resources. Bicep is a DSL that compiles to ARM JSON. Both are idempotent — running the same template repeatedly converges to the same state.
