interview_qa.q: How would you design the Azure management hierarchy (management groups, subscriptions, resource groups) for a large enterprise integration platform?
interview_qa.a: I design top-down based on governance requirements. At the Management Group level, I separate production from non-production because they have fundamentally different governance — prod gets stricter Azure Policy (no public IPs, required tags enforced, specific SKUs only) and more restricted RBAC. Within the production management group, I typically have separate subscriptions for different platforms or major product lines if budget isolation and quota management are concerns — separate subscriptions also provide blast radius isolation. Within a subscription, resource groups follow the lifecycle principle: everything in an integration resource group gets deployed together and deleted together. I separate networking resources into their own resource group because they're managed by a different team with a different change cadence. Within the integration resource group, I group by workload (one resource group per major integration domain — orders, payments, inventory) so teams have clear ownership. I also enforce tagging policy at the management group level: environment, costCenter, owner, application — these feed into cost allocation and are enforced via Azure Policy deny effects.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between High Availability and Disaster Recovery? How do you design for both in a Logic Apps integration scenario?
interview_qa.a: HA is about surviving expected, localized failures — a single VM failing, a single AZ going down, a rolling update taking out an instance. HA is achieved within a single region using redundancy and auto-scaling. DR is about surviving catastrophic events — an entire region going dark, data corruption, accidental deletion. DR requires geographic separation. For Logic Apps Standard: HA is handled by deploying on an App Service Plan with zone redundancy enabled (Premium or Isolated SKU, min 3 instances distributed across AZs). This gives single-AZ failure tolerance. For DR: Logic Apps Standard stores workflow state in Azure Storage — I configure geo-redundant storage (GZRS or RA-GZRS) so workflow run history replicates to a secondary region. For the Logic App itself, I deploy an identical instance in the secondary region using the same IaC template. Service Bus Geo-DR replicates namespace metadata but not in-flight messages. During a failover, I update Traffic Manager or Front Door to route incoming requests to the secondary region's Logic App. The RTO is typically 5-15 minutes for a manual failover with this setup. I also ensure the secondary region's Logic App has its own set of RBAC permissions — a common oversight is forgetting to grant the secondary instance access to shared Key Vaults or downstream services.

interview_qa.q: Explain composite SLA and how it affects your integration architecture decisions.
interview_qa.a: Composite SLA is the combined availability of all components in a critical path — it's the product of individual SLAs. If my integration chain is: APIM (99.95%) → Service Bus Premium (99.9%) → Function App Premium (99.95%) → Azure SQL (99.99%), the composite SLA is 0.9995 × 0.999 × 0.9995 × 0.9999 = approximately 99.84%. So even though each component has a strong SLA, the chain's SLA degrades with each addition. This calculation drives several decisions. First, it justifies using fewer, higher-reliability components — consolidating two separate Service Bus hops into one reduces compounding. Second, it justifies active-active geo-redundancy when the business SLA requires 99.95%+: with two independent regional deployments behind Front Door, the composite SLA calculation changes because a failure in one region doesn't take the whole service down. Third, it justifies asynchronous patterns — if I process requests through a queue rather than synchronously, a temporary backend outage doesn't cause customer-visible failures; messages queue up and drain when the backend recovers. I've done this composite SLA analysis in architecture review documents to get business sign-off on target SLAs before deployment.

interview_qa.q: What is Azure Resource Manager and why does it matter for infrastructure as code and governance?
interview_qa.a: ARM is the control plane for all Azure resources — every operation (create, read, update, delete) goes through ARM regardless of the tool used. This matters for several reasons. First, consistency: whether you use the portal, CLI, Bicep, Terraform, or a custom SDK, the same ARM API is called — ARM enforces RBAC, resource locks, and Azure Policy before any action is taken. Second, infrastructure as code: ARM templates and Bicep describe desired state declaratively. ARM does idempotent deployments — running the same Bicep template multiple times converges to the same state, making it safe for CI/CD pipelines. Third, governance: Azure Policy works through ARM — every resource operation is intercepted by the policy engine. A policy with 'deny' effect will block ARM from completing a non-compliant deployment before the resource is even created. Fourth, resource locks: applied through ARM, they prevent even Owners from accidentally deleting critical resources like production Key Vaults or Service Bus namespaces. In my team's deployment practices, all infrastructure is deployed via Bicep in CI/CD pipelines — no manual portal changes allowed in production. This is enforced by restricting RBAC (only the deployment service principal has Contributor rights in prod).

interview_qa.q: Explain Availability Zones and when zone-redundancy matters vs when it doesn't.
interview_qa.a: Availability Zones are physically separate data centers within a region — separate power, cooling, network — close enough to have low-latency synchronous replication but far enough to be independent failure domains. Zone-redundancy means your resource is replicated across multiple AZs so a single AZ failure doesn't cause outage. This matters when your SLA requires resilience to data center-level failures, which are real but infrequent. It doesn't matter — or rather, isn't sufficient — for DR against regional failures. When I deploy integration services for an enterprise client: Service Bus Premium with zone redundancy on ($1,000+/month) for guaranteed message durability across data center failures; Function App Premium with zone-pinned instances (at least 3, one per AZ) for compute HA; Azure SQL with zone-redundant Business Critical tier for data HA. For lower-criticality integration scenarios (developer tools, event-driven analytics), I often skip AZ redundancy and accept the lower SLA — the cost difference is significant and the risk profile doesn't warrant it. The key is making the RTO/RPO/SLA requirements explicit with the business and then mapping them to architectural choices.

interview_qa.q: How do you use Azure Policy to enforce governance on integration resources?
interview_qa.a: Azure Policy is a declarative rules engine that evaluates resources at deployment time (deny non-compliant) or after (audit and remediate). I use it in several ways for integration governance. First, required tags — a policy requiring costCenter, environment, and owner tags on all resources, with a deny effect so untagged resources can't be deployed. Second, allowed SKUs — a policy limiting Service Bus to Standard or Premium only (no Basic tier) and Function Apps to Premium or Dedicated plan in production (no Consumption plan, which can have cold start issues). Third, network controls — a policy requiring private endpoints on all Service Bus namespaces and Storage accounts in production, and denying public network access. Fourth, diagnostic settings — a policy with 'deployIfNotExists' effect that automatically deploys diagnostic settings to Log Analytics on any new Service Bus namespace or Function App. This ensures everything is monitored without relying on humans remembering to configure it. I define policies in JSON or Bicep, assign them at the management group level for production, and test them in our dev subscription first. Initiatives are collections of related policies — I package all integration governance policies into a single initiative for easy assignment.

interview_qa.q: What is an Azure Resource Lock and when would you apply one?
interview_qa.a: Azure Resource Locks prevent modification or deletion of resources even by users with Owner RBAC role — you have to explicitly remove the lock before you can modify or delete. Two types: CanNotDelete allows reads and updates but prevents deletion. ReadOnly prevents any modifications or deletions (effectively puts the resource in a read-only state). I apply CanNotDelete locks to: production Key Vaults (accidental deletion loses all secrets/certs — catastrophic), Service Bus namespaces with active subscriptions (deletion loses in-flight messages), Log Analytics workspaces (loss of historical logs breaks compliance and debugging). I don't apply ReadOnly locks to resources that change frequently — it breaks many ARM operations like restarting a Function App or scaling a Service Bus. In our organization, CanNotDelete locks on production resources is an Azure Policy requirement — the 'deployIfNotExists' effect automatically applies the lock after resource creation. Lock management (the ability to remove locks) is granted only to a handful of senior engineers and the break-glass emergency account.

interview_qa.q: How do you calculate and communicate RTO and RPO for an integration service, and what Azure architectures correspond to different targets?
interview_qa.a: RTO is the maximum time the business can tolerate the service being down; RPO is the maximum data loss (in time) acceptable. I start by interviewing stakeholders: 'If this integration is down, what business process stops? How long until that costs real money or violates an SLA?' That gives the RTO. 'If we lose messages from the last X hours, can we recover them from source systems?' gives the RPO. Typical tiers I've used: Hot Standby (active-active) for RTO < 5 minutes, RPO near-zero: two fully deployed regional instances behind Azure Front Door, Service Bus Geo-DR premium, GZRS storage. Most expensive. Warm Standby for RTO 15-60 minutes, RPO < 15 minutes: secondary region deployed but scaled to zero or minimal capacity, automated failover scripts, geo-redundant storage. Cost-effective. Cold Standby for RTO 4-24 hours, RPO hours: IaC templates stored in a secondary region, periodic data exports, manual recovery runbook. Cheapest. For most enterprise integration services I design warm standby — the premium cost of hot-standby active-active is hard to justify unless the integration touches revenue in real time. I document RTO/RPO targets, the architecture that achieves them, and the runbook for failover in our integration operations guide.

talking_points: In one of my EY projects, I led the governance design for an Azure integration platform serving 8 business units. I designed a management group hierarchy that separated production from non-production and applied Azure Policy at the management group level — required tagging, private endpoint enforcement, and automatic diagnostic settings deployment. This reduced misconfigured resources in production to near zero.

I've done composite SLA analysis for integration architects at client sites who didn't understand why their 99.9% SLA commitment to the business was unachievable with their 7-component synchronous chain. The math clearly showed the need to move to async patterns and at least two of the critical components to zone-redundant SKUs.

I designed and tested a disaster recovery failover for a Service Bus-based integration platform. The runbook covered: failing over Service Bus Geo-DR alias, updating Front Door origin weights to shift traffic to the secondary region, verifying managed identity permissions in the secondary region, and confirming message processing resumed. We achieved RTO of 12 minutes in our DR drill.

I enforce no manual production deployments through a combination of RBAC (only the deployment service principal has Contributor; humans have Reader) and resource locks (CanNotDelete on critical resources). Every change goes through a Bicep deployment in our Azure DevOps pipeline with mandatory review. This eliminated accidental configuration drift.

When asked about availability zones, I always clarify: AZs protect against data center failures within a region; they don't protect against region-wide failures. I've had to educate clients who thought deploying zone-redundant was their DR strategy — it's not. DR needs a second region. Both are needed for a complete availability story.

topic5.title: Azure Storage
topic5.concepts:
Azure Blob Storage is an object storage service for unstructured data — files, images, videos, logs, backups, and large payload files in integration scenarios. The hierarchy is: Storage Account → Container → Blob. Containers are like folders but flat (no real subdirectories, though path prefixes simulate them). Blobs come in three types: Block Blobs (general purpose, up to ~190 TB, uploaded in blocks and committed — most common), Append Blobs (optimized for append operations — good for logging), and Page Blobs (for random read/write access — Azure Disk storage uses this). Access tiers (only on Block Blobs): Hot (frequently accessed, highest storage cost, lowest access cost), Cool (infrequently accessed, lower storage cost, higher access cost, 30-day minimum), Cold (even lower storage, higher access, 90-day minimum), Archive (lowest storage cost, highest retrieval cost and latency — must be 'rehydrated' before access, takes hours). Lifecycle management policies automatically move blobs between tiers or delete them based on age.

Shared Access Signatures (SAS) provide delegated, time-limited access to specific resources in a storage account without sharing the account key. An Account SAS grants access to one or more services (Blob, Queue, Table). A Service SAS grants access to a specific service. A User Delegation SAS is the most secure form — it uses your Entra ID credentials to sign the SAS rather than the account key, meaning it can be revoked immediately by revoking the delegating user's permissions. SAS parameters include: resource (container, blob), permissions (read, write, delete, list), start/expiry time, allowed IP ranges, and required protocol (HTTPS only). In integration scenarios, SAS tokens are used when you need to give an external party temporary access to a blob (e.g., sharing a generated report) or when a downstream service needs to upload/download directly without going through your application.

Azure Queue Storage and Azure Service Bus Queues are both message queuing services but with different characteristics. Azure Queue Storage is simple, cheap, and massively scalable (up to 500 TB per queue) — it stores messages up to 64KB, delivers them at-least-once, and has no ordering guarantee. Maximum visibility timeout is 7 days. It's appropriate for high-volume, simple decoupling where messages are idempotent and ordering doesn't matter. Service Bus Queues are the enterprise messaging tier: messages up to 256KB (Standard) or 100MB (Premium), FIFO guarantee via sessions, duplicate detection (exactly-once processing), dead-letter queue, message deferral, scheduled delivery, topic/subscription fan-out, and transactions. Service Bus also supports the AMQP 1.0 protocol. For integration architectures, Service Bus is almost always the right choice unless the use case is very high volume, simple, and the messages are idempotent — then Storage Queue's cost advantage matters.

Azure Table Storage is a NoSQL key-value store. The data model has Partition Key + Row Key as the composite primary key. Partition Key determines which partition (and therefore which storage node) holds the data — queries that filter by Partition Key are efficient; cross-partition queries are scanned across all partitions. Good for: structured but flexible data (no fixed schema), high-volume reads/writes at low cost, and time-series data where Partition Key is the time bucket and Row Key is a unique identifier. Table Storage doesn't support secondary indexes, transactions across partitions, or joins. Azure Cosmos DB Table API is the premium upgrade if you need global distribution, multiple secondary indexes, or stronger consistency guarantees. In integration architecture, Table Storage is used for: storing deduplication state (have we seen this message before?), tracking processing state of workflow steps, or simple audit logs.

Azure Managed Disks are block storage for virtual machines — they're not relevant to PaaS integration services like Logic Apps or Function Apps directly, but they matter when you run integration components on IaaS VMs or when you're containerizing workloads on AKS. Managed Disks have SKUs: Ultra Disk (high IOPS, low latency for databases), Premium SSD (production VMs), Standard SSD, and Standard HDD. They differ from Blob Storage fundamentally: Managed Disks are attached to VMs as block devices (like a physical hard drive); Blob Storage is accessed via HTTPS. In the context of Azure Files (which is storage mounted as SMB/NFS shares), this matters if migrating on-premises file-based integrations that expect a mapped drive letter. Azure Files can be mounted by Logic Apps Standard and Azure Functions (Premium plan) for shared file system access.

terms.definition: Top-level Azure Storage resource. Has a unique name (DNS: <name>.blob.core.windows.net). Contains Blob containers, Queues, Tables, and File Shares. Redundancy options: LRS, ZRS, GRS, GZRS, RA-GRS, RA-GZRS.
Individual stored object in Blob Storage. Types: Block Blob (general), Append Blob (log-style), Page Blob (random access, used by managed disks). Max size per block blob: ~190 TB.
Logical grouping of blobs within a storage account. Analogous to a folder at the root level. Public access can be set per container (private, blob, container).

Blob cost optimization setting. Hot (frequent access), Cool (infrequent, 30-day min), Cold (90-day min), Archive (offline retrieval required, hours latency). Lifecycle policies automate tier transitions.
Rules-based automation for blobs: transition to cooler tiers or delete after N days since last modified or since creation. Reduces storage costs for log archives and processed files.
Signed URL or header granting time-limited, permission-scoped access to storage resources. Types: Account SAS, Service SAS, User Delegation SAS (most secure, signed with Entra ID credentials).

LRS (3 copies in one datacenter), ZRS (3 copies across AZs), GRS (LRS + async replication to paired region), GZRS (ZRS + async replication to paired region), RA-GRS/RA-GZRS (same + read access to secondary).
Simple, cheap, scalable message queue. Messages up to 64KB. At-least-once delivery. No ordering guarantee. Max 500TB per queue. Good for high-volume, idempotent workloads.
Enterprise messaging broker. Queues and Topics/Subscriptions. FIFO via sessions, duplicate detection, dead-letter queue, message deferral, 256KB-100MB messages. AMQP 1.0 and HTTPS protocols.
Sub-queue in Service Bus holding messages that failed processing after max delivery attempts, expired, or were explicitly dead-lettered by the consumer. Used for error investigation and reprocessing.
NoSQL key-value store. Partition Key + Row Key composite key. No schema, no joins, no secondary indexes. Fast for single-partition queries. Good for high-volume structured data at low cost.
Block storage for VMs. SKUs: Ultra, Premium SSD, Standard SSD, Standard HDD. Managed by Azure (no storage account management). Distinct from Blob Storage.
Fully managed SMB/NFS file share in Azure. Mountable by Windows, Linux, and macOS. Can be mounted to Logic Apps Standard and Function App Premium for shared file system access.
Restrict storage account access to specific VNets (service endpoints) or private IPs (private endpoints). Service Endpoints route traffic over Azure backbone but source IP remains in VNet range.

Blob Storage feature implementing WORM (Write Once Read Many) via time-based retention policies or legal holds. Used for regulatory compliance — blobs cannot be modified or deleted during the retention period.

interview_qa.q: Explain Azure Blob Storage access tiers and how you use lifecycle management in an integration architecture.
interview_qa.a: Blob Storage has four access tiers: Hot for data you access frequently (highest storage cost, lowest retrieval cost), Cool for data accessed less than once a month (lower storage, higher retrieval, 30-day minimum retention before tier change), Cold for data accessed even less frequently (90-day minimum), and Archive for data you rarely access (cheapest storage, but blobs are offline — you must issue a rehydration request that takes hours before you can read the data). In integration architectures, I use tiered storage for file archival: large EDI and XML files come in through SFTP/SFTP Logic App connectors and land in a Hot container. After processing, they're moved to a processed container and a Lifecycle Management policy transitions them to Cool after 30 days (we might need to re-retrieve them for reconciliation), then Archive after 90 days (compliance archive only), then delete after 7 years (regulatory retention). This cuts storage costs by 80%+ for a high-volume EDI customer who was accumulating TBs of processed files in Hot tier unnecessarily. I always set the minimum days before tier change to respect the minimum billing periods to avoid unexpected costs.

interview_qa.q: When would you use a Shared Access Signature vs Managed Identity for storage access?
interview_qa.a: Managed Identity is always preferred for internal Azure services — there's no credential to manage, rotate, or accidentally expose. When a Function App, Logic App, or APIM needs to access a storage account, I configure Managed Identity with 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' RBAC role and use Azure SDKs with DefaultAzureCredential. No connection strings, no account keys. SAS tokens are for two scenarios: first, when you need to give an external party temporary access to a specific blob or container without giving them access to your Azure environment. For example, generating a download link for a generated report for a partner — I generate a User Delegation SAS (signed with Entra ID, not the account key, so it can be revoked), scoped to the specific blob, read-only, expiring in 1 hour, HTTPS-only. Second, for browser-based uploads where the client needs to write directly to Blob Storage (bypassing your backend). A Service SAS with write permission and a 15-minute window allows the browser to upload large files directly to blob, avoiding the backend from being a bottleneck. I never use Account SAS — it's too broad. And I never expose storage account keys; those are treated as root credentials and stored only in Key Vault.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between Azure Queue Storage and Service Bus, and when do you choose each in an integration design?
interview_qa.a: The choice comes down to message complexity, ordering requirements, and volume. Storage Queue is optimized for simplicity and scale: messages up to 64KB, at-least-once delivery, no ordering, no dead-lettering, cheap (fractions of a cent per million operations), and can hold up to 500TB of messages. Service Bus Queue is the enterprise messaging tier: messages up to 256KB on Standard or 100MB on Premium, FIFO guarantee through sessions, duplicate detection windows, dead-letter queues, message deferral, scheduled delivery, and Topics/Subscriptions for pub-sub fan-out. In integration architecture, I use Service Bus for: order processing where order matters, financial transactions where duplicate processing is catastrophic, workflows where individual steps can fail and need dead-lettering for investigation, and any scenario requiring at-least-once with deduplication approaching exactly-once semantics. I use Storage Queue for: high-volume event notifications where messages are idempotent by design (each message is a full state snapshot, not a delta), ingesting telemetry at massive scale where duplicates are filtered downstream, or decoupling components where the downstream processes at most once per cycle regardless of duplicates.

interview_qa.q: How does a dead-letter queue work in Service Bus, and what is your operational process for handling DLQ messages?
interview_qa.a: A dead-letter queue (DLQ) is a sub-queue automatically created for every Service Bus queue and topic subscription, named with a $DeadLetterQueue suffix. Messages end up in the DLQ when: they exceed the MaxDeliveryCount (default 10 attempts — message couldn't be processed despite retries), the message's TimeToLive expires before it's consumed, or the consumer explicitly dead-letters it via DeadLetter() with a reason code (useful for business validation failures you want to investigate). DLQ messages persist and don't expire — they sit there until you process or delete them. My operational process: I set up Azure Monitor alerts when DLQ message count exceeds a threshold. For investigation, I use Service Bus Explorer or a dedicated tool to peek at DLQ messages without removing them. I classify DLQ messages by reason: transient failures (infrastructure issue — safe to reprocess) vs. poison messages (bad data — need human review and likely source system fix). I have a Function App that reads from the DLQ, logs the message and reason to Log Analytics, and notifies the on-call engineer. For transient failures, we have a runbook to re-enqueue the messages to the main queue. I never delete DLQ messages without logging them — they're evidence of failures and often reveal data quality issues upstream.

interview_qa.q: How do you secure a storage account used in an integration architecture — what are the layers of security?
interview_qa.a: Defense in depth for storage: First, disable shared key access — require Entra ID authentication and Managed Identity. This prevents any connection string-based access even if an account key leaks. Second, network restrictions: configure the storage firewall to deny public access, allowing only specific VNet subnets (via Service Endpoints) or specific private endpoints. For production integration, always use Private Endpoints — traffic never leaves the Microsoft backbone. Third, disable public blob access at the account level — no container can be set to anonymous access. Fourth, enable Soft Delete for blobs (30-day recovery window) and containers (7-day recovery) to protect against accidental or malicious deletion. Fifth, enable diagnostic logging to Log Analytics — all access events, failures, and authentication events are logged. Sixth, configure immutable storage policies on compliance-critical containers (audit logs, legal records). Seventh, RBAC assignments at the narrowest scope: individual containers where possible, not the storage account, using data-plane roles (Storage Blob Data Contributor) not control-plane roles (Contributor). Eighth, Microsoft Defender for Storage to detect anomalous access patterns and malware in uploaded files. I've implemented all of these as a Bicep module that any team can consume — secure by default, with documented overrides for specific use cases.

interview_qa.q: Explain Azure Table Storage's data model and when you would use it in an integration context.
interview_qa.a: Table Storage uses a key-value model with three mandatory system properties: PartitionKey, RowKey (together forming the unique key), and Timestamp. Entity properties can be arbitrary key-value pairs with no enforced schema across entities. The PartitionKey is the distribution unit — all entities with the same PartitionKey live on the same storage partition and can be read/written with single-partition transactions. RowKey uniquely identifies an entity within a partition. In integration, I use Table Storage for: deduplication state tracking (PartitionKey = message type, RowKey = message ID, entity = received timestamp; check before processing to skip duplicates). Distributed locks (PartitionKey = resource name, RowKey = 'lock', use optimistic concurrency via ETag to prevent race conditions). Checkpoint tracking (PartitionKey = workflow ID, RowKey = step name, entity = status + timestamp — good for long-running batch processing). API call caching (PartitionKey = cache namespace, RowKey = cache key, entity = cached response + expiry). I prefer Table Storage over SQL for these patterns when: there's no relational structure needed, query patterns are always by partition key, and cost needs to be minimal. For anything requiring complex queries, joins, or ACID transactions across entities — I use Cosmos DB or Azure SQL instead.

interview_qa.q: What is geo-redundant storage (GRS) and how does it affect your integration recovery strategy?
interview_qa.a: GRS replicates your storage data asynchronously to a paired secondary region. There are variants: GRS (LRS in primary + async copy to LRS in secondary — read access only during failover), RA-GRS (same + read access to secondary at all times, secondary endpoint has '-secondary' suffix), GZRS (ZRS in primary + async copy to LRS in secondary — combines zone redundancy with geo-redundancy), RA-GZRS (same + read access to secondary). The async replication means there's a lag — the RPO for geo-redundant storage is typically less than 15 minutes but not zero. For integration recovery: Logic Apps Standard stores workflow state in Azure Storage. If I configure GZRS on that storage account, workflow run history is geo-replicated with zone redundancy in the primary. In a regional failover, I can failover the storage account (customer-initiated failover — the secondary becomes the primary), then redeploy the Logic App in the secondary region pointing to the now-primary storage. I've designed recovery runbooks that use RA-GZRS so that even before failover, the secondary region's Logic App can read historical run data from the secondary storage endpoint — useful for read-only investigation during an incident without needing to trigger a full failover.

interview_qa.q: How do you use Azure Blob Storage in an event-driven integration pipeline with Event Grid?
interview_qa.a: Blob Storage natively integrates with Event Grid — you can configure a Storage Event subscription that fires events when blobs are created, deleted, or renamed in specific containers. These events are pushed as JSON payloads to Event Grid, which routes them to subscribers. In a file processing integration: a partner drops files into a Blob container via SFTP (via Logic App SFTP connector that writes to Blob) or via SAS URL. The blob creation fires a Microsoft.Storage.BlobCreated event to Event Grid. Event Grid routes the event to a Logic App HTTP endpoint or a Function App trigger. The consumer reads the blob URL from the event payload, processes the file, writes results to another container, and moves or deletes the original. Key design considerations: (1) Filter events in the Event Grid subscription by blob name prefix and suffix — if partners put files in different paths, you can route to different processors. (2) Handle the event idempotently — Event Grid delivers at-least-once; your Function may receive the same BlobCreated event twice. Check if the blob has already been processed (Table Storage dedup). (3) Set a dead-letter destination on the Event Grid subscription — if your subscriber is down, undeliverable events go to a Blob container or Service Bus dead-letter queue. I've built this pattern for multiple clients replacing legacy FTP polling schedulers.

talking_points: I replaced a BizTalk file adapter polling solution with an Event Grid + Blob Storage integration: partners upload to Blob via SAS URL, Blob events trigger Event Grid, Event Grid fans out to Logic Apps processors. Processing latency dropped from 5 minutes (poll interval) to under 30 seconds, and we eliminated the polling infrastructure entirely.

I implemented a lifecycle management policy that saved a client 60% on storage costs. They had 12TB of processed EDI files in Hot tier that were never accessed after processing. I moved them to Cool at 30 days and Archive at 90 days, keeping compliance retention for 7 years at minimal cost.

When designing deduplication for a high-throughput messaging pipeline, I used Table Storage as a distributed dedup store: PartitionKey = message type, RowKey = message ID, TTL-simulated by deleting old entities. It handled 10,000 checks per minute at a fraction of the cost of using Cosmos DB or Redis for this purpose.

I operationalized our DLQ process by building a Function App that monitors all Service Bus DLQs in our namespace, logs every DLQ message to Log Analytics with reason codes, and sends an alert to our PagerDuty integration if DLQ count exceeds 10 in 5 minutes. Before this, DLQ messages would sit undetected for days.

I hardened all storage accounts in our integration platform by enabling Defender for Storage and catching a malware upload attempt within the first month. A partner was infected and accidentally uploaded an executable. Defender flagged it immediately and we quarantined the blob before it was processed by any downstream Function. That was a real wake-up call on why file upload pipelines need AV scanning.
